data.title: Alphabet Teams Up With Sea on AI for Shopee, Garena
data.description:
Alphabet (GOOGL, Financials) said its Google unit has formed a strategic partnership with Sea Ltd to develop artificial intelligence tools for Sea’s e-commerce and gaming businesses.

Under the agreement, the companies will explore building an AI “agentic shopping” prototype for Sea’s Shopee platform, according to a joint statement. The initiative aims to expand AI capabilities beyond basic queries to more complex shopping and workflow tasks.

Shopee was Southeast Asia’s largest e-commerce platform in 2024 with a 52% market share, according to consultancy Momentum Works. The new AI features could strengthen its position as competition intensifies, including from Alibaba’s Lazada platform.

Google and Sea’s gaming arm Garena will also deploy AI solutions to improve productivity in game development. The companies said the tools are intended to streamline development processes and enhance user engagement.

The partnership follows a 2024 collaboration between Shopee and YouTube in the region’s e-commerce market.

data.title:
Michael Burry Sounds Alarm on Palantir -- Warns Shares Could Fall to $46
        (Status: Pending)
data.description:
Palantir Technologies (PLTR, Financial) is again under pressure after investor Michael Burry (Trades, Portfolio) released a detailed report suggesting the stock may be overvalued. Burry said Palantir's recent gains might not last. He outlined a scenario where the stock could fall to around $46, down sharply from recent levels, following a 26% drop in the past few weeks.

The investor questioned how Palantir allocates labor costs during software deployments, arguing it inflates profit margins and makes the company look more profitable than it is. He also critiqued leadership and corporate culture, suggesting market enthusiasm is tied more to story than fundamentals.

Wall Street analysts disagreed. D.A. Davidson's Gil Luria said the report doesn't change his outlook, highlighting Palantir's rapid growth and a 137% jump in U.S. commercial sales.

Palantir expects 2026 revenue of $7.19 billion, up about 60% from last year, with strong cash reserves and low debt providing a buffer against market swings.

Technical analysts flagged chart patterns hinting at possible further downside, with initial support levels lower than current trading prices around $131.

data.title: Nvidia Sells Final Arm Stake, Unloads 1.1 Million Shares
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA, Financial) has moved to formally close a chapter that once reshaped the semiconductor landscape. A recent regulatory filing shows the company sold 1.1 million shares of Arm Holdings (ARM, Financial) in the fourth quarter of last year, valued at roughly $140 million based on Arm's Tuesday closing price, reducing its stake to zero. Nvidia declined to comment, and Arm did not immediately respond, but the transaction effectively severs the last remaining equity link between the two companies following a deal that unraveled several years ago.

Investors will remember that Nvidia agreed in 2020 to acquire Arm for $40 billion in what was expected to become the largest transaction in chip industry history. The proposal quickly drew scrutiny from regulators and customers, many of whom viewed Arm's independence as critical given that its technology underpins most of the world's advanced semiconductors. In February 2022, the companies terminated the agreement, and Arm, majority-owned by SoftBank Group (SOBKY, Financial), proceeded with plans to sell shares to the public, reshaping its path as a standalone entity.

The exit also comes as Nvidia, now the world's most valuable company, continues to deploy capital across the broader technology ecosystem. The company holds stakes in Intel (INTC), Nokia (NOK, Financial), CoreWeave (CRWV, Financial) and Synopsys (SNPS, Financial), and has indicated it intends to use its cash to accelerate the adoption of artificial intelligence computing. Against that backdrop, divesting the final Arm position could simplify Nvidia's investment portfolio and potentially sharpen its focus on AI-driven opportunities, a theme that remains central to its long-term capital allocation strategy.

data.title: Japan Launches Up to $36 Billion US Energy Investment Under $550 Billion Pact
data.description:
Japan's long-trailed $550 billion investment framework with the US is now starting to translate into tangible projects, with up to $36 billion earmarked for oil, gas and critical mineral developments in the first tranche of funding. President Donald Trump characterized the launch as the start of a “MASSIVE Trade Deal,” linking the investment drive to the 15% tariff rate agreed on Japanese products, including reduced duties on automobiles. Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi framed the initiative as a way to reinforce economic security and resilient supply chains across energy, artificial intelligence and critical minerals, suggesting Tokyo is positioning capital toward areas viewed as strategically important while still seeking stable returns.

The anchor project is a natural gas facility in Ohio expected to generate 9.2 gigawatts of power. According to the Commerce Department fact sheet, Japan could invest up to $33 billion in the plant, which will be led by a subsidiary of SoftBank Group Corp., with Toshiba Corp. and Hitachi Ltd. expressing interest in participating. If the facility operates at full capacity, it would be equivalent to nine nuclear reactors or roughly the power consumption of about 7.4 million homes on the PJM Interconnection grid. The timing could be notable, as demand from new data centers tied to the artificial intelligence buildout has increased pressure on US energy supply planning. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said the structure is designed so Japan provides the capital, the infrastructure is built in the US, and proceeds are arranged so Japan earns its return while the US expands strategic industrial capacity.

Additional projects include a $2.1 billion investment in the Texas GulfLink deepwater crude export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico, to be operated by Sentinel Midstream, which the Commerce Department said could generate as much as $30 billion in annual US crude exports at full capacity. Japan is also expected to invest $600 million in a high-pressure, high-temperature synthetic industrial diamond grit facility in Georgia involving Element Six, a subsidiary of De Beers, producing materials used in semiconductor, automotive and energy manufacturing. Financing is expected to involve the Japan Bank for International Cooperation and Nippon Export and Investment Insurance, with most of the broader $550 billion mechanism structured around loans and guarantees rather than direct cash investment. With Takaichi scheduled to meet Trump in Washington on March 19, implementation of these projects could become a focal point as both sides work to move from political commitments to operational execution.

data.title: Treasuries Extend Rally; 10-Year Yield Slips to 4.03%
data.description:
Last week's rally in US Treasuries rolled straight into Tuesday as the bond market reopened after the holiday, reinforcing a narrative that inflation may be cooling enough to shift the policy backdrop. The benchmark 10-year yield slipped two basis points to 4.03%, while the two-year yield edged toward its lowest level since 2022 in thin Asian trading. The move builds on a strong finish to last week, when softer US CPI data fueled expectations that the Federal Reserve could cut interest rates at least twice this year, a potential pivot that may recalibrate positioning across rates and risk assets.

Prashant Newnaha, senior strategist at TD Securities in Singapore, attributed the bid to softer inflation data and ongoing systematic quant fund deleveraging in equities. He flagged 4% on the US 10-year as a make-or-break level, suggesting a sharper rally could unfold if that threshold is breached. At the same time, US equity futures retreated, underscoring how falling yields can reflect both policy optimism and caution around growth. For investors, the technical setup around 4% may now act as a near-term inflection point for duration exposure.

The strength in bonds extended across the region. Australian and New Zealand yields posted modest declines, while Japanese government bonds rallied after demand at a five-year auction showed signs of stabilization. Traders are now focused on further US labor market data and the minutes from the Fed's January meeting for clearer signals on the timing of any potential shift in interest rates. Those releases could shape whether the current momentum in global bond markets continues or begins to consolidate around these closely watched levels.

data.title: BHP Profit Jumps 22% as Copper Delivers Over Half of Earnings
data.description:
BHP Group (BHP, Financial) reported earnings at the upper end of analyst expectations, as its multi-year pivot toward copper combined with a historic metals rally to cushion a softer phase in iron ore. Underlying attributable profit rose 22% to $6.2 billion for the six months to the end of December, while shares climbed as much as 7.6% in Sydney to a record, marking the biggest gain in more than 10 months. Chief Executive Officer Mike Henry framed the shift as deliberate, noting that copper accounted for more than half of group profit for the first time — a milestone that could reflect how diversification efforts are beginning to reshape the earnings mix.

The copper division delivered a 59% increase in underlying EBITDA to $8 billion, supported by steady output and prices that climbed by almost a third over the period. Improvements at Escondida, the world's largest copper mine, along with prior acquisitions, contributed to the stronger result. By contrast, earnings from iron ore edged 4% higher, even as “tough” negotiations continue with China Mineral Resources Group Co. Henry described BHP's broader relationships with steel mills as “deep and strategic” and said discussions with CMRG were constructive, adding that the company is confident a mutually acceptable resolution can be reached.

Looking ahead, management continues to emphasize capital discipline and portfolio optimization. The Jansen potash project in Canada remains on track for first production in the middle of next year, although first-phase investment is now estimated at $8.4 billion after further cost increases. BHP also announced a $4.3 billion long-term silver streaming agreement with Wheaton Precious Metals Corp., tied to by-product output from the Antamina copper mine in Peru, where it holds a 33.75% stake, following the recent sale of a $2 billion interest in the Pilbara power network. An interim dividend of 73 cents, equivalent to a 60% payout ratio, underscores management's stated focus on reinvesting in priority areas or potentially returning additional capital to shareholders, particularly as large-scale acquisition attempts have proved difficult and organic growth remains central to the strategy.

data.title: VAALCO Energy (EGY) Maintains Consistent Dividend with 5.07% Yield
data.description:
lang="en">

VAALCO Energy Financial Analysis

Key Takeaways

  • VAALCO Energy (EGY, Financial) maintains a consistent quarterly dividend, reflecting a forward yield of 5.07%.
  • The company operates primarily in the oil and gas sector, with significant revenue from its Gabon segment.
  • Current financial metrics indicate a mixed outlook with strong operating margins but potential financial distress signals.

Dividend Announcement and Company Overview

VAALCO Energy (EGY) has announced a quarterly dividend of $0.0625 per share, maintaining its consistent payout for the thirteenth consecutive quarter. The dividend, offering a forward yield of 5.07%, is payable on March 27 to shareholders recorded by February 27, with the ex-dividend date also on February 27. The company's steadfast dividend policy underscores its commitment to shareholder returns.

VAALCO Energy Inc is an independent energy company primarily engaged in the acquisition, exploration, development, and production of crude oil and natural gas. The company operates through geographical segments including Gabon, Egypt, Canada, Equatorial Guinea, and Cote d'Ivoire, with the majority of its revenue generated from the Gabon segment. VAALCO Energy is positioned within the energy sector, specifically in the oil and gas industry, with a market capitalization of approximately $513.99 million.

Financial Health Analysis

VAALCO Energy's financial performance presents a mixed picture. The company reported a revenue of $421.83 million, with a three-year revenue growth rate of 10.9%. Despite this growth, the company's earnings growth has been negative, with a one-year decline of 93.3% and a three-year decline of 14.4%.

Profitability metrics show a robust operating margin of 19.63% and an EBITDA margin of 44.81%, indicating efficient operations. However, the net margin stands at a modest 6.85%, reflecting challenges in translating revenue into net income.

On the balance sheet, VAALCO Energy maintains a current ratio of 1.05 and a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.29, suggesting a stable financial position. However, the Altman Z-Score of 1.71 places the company in the distress zone, indicating potential financial difficulties.

Valuation and Market Sentiment

VAALCO Energy's valuation metrics reveal a P/E ratio of 18.26, which is close to its five-year high, suggesting that the stock may be overvalued relative to its historical range. The P/S ratio of 1.21 and P/B ratio of 1.02 are also near their one-year highs.

Analyst sentiment is cautiously optimistic, with a target price of $8.10 and a recommendation score of 2, indicating a moderate buy. Technical indicators such as the RSI of 58.23 suggest that the stock is neither overbought nor oversold, while moving averages indicate a positive trend.

Institutional ownership stands at 58.43%, with insider ownership at 9.43%. Recent insider activity shows minimal transactions, with one insider sell in the past 12 months.

Risk Assessment

VAALCO Energy's financial health grades highlight several risks. The Piotroski F-Score of 3 indicates poor business operations, while the Beneish M-Score of -2.9 suggests that the company is unlikely to be manipulating its financial statements.

Sector-specific risks include volatility in oil and gas prices, which can impact revenue and profitability. The company's beta of 0.87 indicates lower volatility compared to the market, but investors should remain cautious given the inherent risks in the energy sector.

Overall, while VAALCO Energy shows strong operational efficiency, potential financial distress signals and valuation concerns warrant careful consideration by investors.

data.title: Costco Sued Over Rotisserie Chicken Claims
data.description:
Costco (COST, Financial) is dealing with fresh legal pressure after a nonprofit backed lawsuit alleged salmonella contamination at the company's Nebraska chicken processing plant, raising concerns about one of its most iconic products.

The proposed class action, filed Thursday in Seattle federal court and cited by Reuters, points to a December study and claims Costco failed to properly control salmonella risks in its chicken supply. The plaintiff, Lisa Taylor of Missouri, said she typically bought 1 to 2 rotisserie chickens a month at St. Louis-area stores and believes she overpaid because potential contamination risks were not disclosed.

This is not just any product. Costco sold more than 157 million rotisserie chickens worldwide in 2025, according to remarks at its annual meeting. The $4.99 chicken has long been a symbol of the retailer's value proposition and a key driver of store traffic.

data.title: SpaceX Shifts To Moon, Challenges Bezos
data.description:
SpaceX is changing course in a way few expected. After years of talking about Mars as the ultimate destination, Tesla (TSLA, Financial) CEO Elon Musk now says the company will prioritize building a “self-growing city” on the Moon, putting him on a more direct collision path with Amazon (AMZN, Financial) CEO Jeff Bezos and Blue Origin.

According to The Wall Street Journal, Musk explained the pivot as a practical decision. A lunar base, he argued, could become self-sustaining in less than 10 years, while Mars would likely take 20+ years due to its 26-month launch windows. He also raised the risk that a natural or manmade catastrophe could interrupt supply missions from Earth, making a nearer-term lunar strategy more resilient.

That shift brings SpaceX closer to Blue Origin's long-held vision. Bezos has consistently pushed the idea of lunar industry and infrastructure, and Blue plans its first cargo moon landing early this year. In response to the news, Bezos posted a tortoise image, a quiet nod to patience.
